userNote: NO MIGRATION FRAMEWORK. ST8_SCHEMA is one 150-line template literal applied via `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`. Columns added after the initial DDL — needsAIReview, tripleAtCount, aiContentInjected, templateVariables, hasUnfilledVariables — will NOT land in any existing st8.sqlite. There is no _migrations table, no version tracking, no idempotent migration runner. Today's workflow is 'delete st8.sqlite or write a one-off ALTER'. Raised independently across clusters; flagging here from the persistence-source angle as P1. Fix: introduce a numbered-migration framework (e.g. /home/user/st8/src/core/database/migrations/00X_name.sql) with an applied-migrations table populated at boot.
actionsTaken:
Deferred — this is the canonical P1.1 roadmap item, not a Wave-1B-shaped ticket. The migration framework is a multi-day system that must (a) define a numbered-migration file layout under src/core/database/migrations/, (b) add a _migrations table tracking applied version + timestamp, (c) wire an applyMigrations() step into St8Persistence.initialize() that runs between the bare ST8_SCHEMA apply and the introspection diff, (d) ship an `npm run db:migrate` CLI for manual application, and (e) author migration 001 to fold the five post-initial columns (needsAIReview, tripleAtCount, aiContentInjected, templateVariables, hasUnfilledVariables) into an ALTER sequence so legacy DBs gain them.

Roadmap reference: docs/_pending-roadmap/persistence-and-database.md:9 (P1.1 — SQLite migration framework). The roadmap's surface-area bullet list is the spec.

What Wave 1A already shipped that makes this clean to build on:
  - EXPECTED_SCHEMA + introspectSchema() at persistence.js (L245+, L431+) — the natural assertion surface after each migration step. The framework should call introspectSchema() after running migrations and throw on residual drift.
  - PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON at boot (persistence.js initialize, post-WAL). Migrations that ALTER tables under FK enforcement must either be FK-safe by construction (ADD COLUMN is, sqlite's 12-step recreate is) or wrap in `PRAGMA foreign_keys = OFF` for the transaction and re-enable after — document the chosen idiom in the migration harness header.
  - deleteTicketsForFile cascade pattern (persistence.js L659, wired into deleteFile + pruneFilesNotIn) — the template for any new FK'd table a migration introduces.

No half-implementation here would be honest. The migration framework needs to land as one focused PR (its own batch in the bible), not bolted onto a sub-wave.
residualConcerns: Until P1.1 lands, every new column added to ST8_SCHEMA after this date will silently NOT appear on existing st8.sqlite files. Workaround remains 'delete st8.sqlite or apply a one-off ALTER'. The introspection diff will surface the gap as `[st8:persistence:drift]` warnings at boot — Wave 1A's payoff — but contributors must read those warnings.
reviewNote: Deferral confirmed legitimate. Opened docs/_pending-roadmap/persistence-and-database.md:9 — line 9 is exactly the P1.1 'SQLite migration framework' header, and the surface-area bullet list (numbered migration files, _migrations table, applyMigrations() step, db:migrate CLI, migration 001 to fold the five post-initial columns) matches the actionsTaken description nearly verbatim. This is roadmap-shaped multi-day work, not Wave-1B-sized: a half-implementation would leave the codebase worse than the deferral. The actionsTaken also correctly identifies the three Wave 1A surfaces the framework should integrate with (EXPECTED_SCHEMA/introspectSchema for post-step assertion, the FK-ON pragma's implication that migrations must be FK-safe or wrap in PRAGMA OFF, deleteTicketsForFile as the cascade-pattern template). residualConcerns honestly notes the workaround that contributors must keep using. Not a dodge — the correct call.

userNote: deleteFile(filepath) is FK-unsafe at scale and has NO warning comment. file_registry permits multiple rows per filepath (different birthTimestamp → different fingerprint), but deleteFile picks the first match via getFileByPath (no ORDER BY), cascades only that one fingerprint's children, then `DELETE FROM file_registry WHERE filepath = ?` removes BOTH rows. Connections/intent/mutation_log rows for the other fingerprint(s) become orphans. The JSDoc on pruneFilesNotIn (L298-313) is the only place in the codebase that records this fact. Fix: add a prominent caveat comment on deleteFile, or rewrite it to operate per-fingerprint like pruneFilesNotIn.
actionsTaken:
Rewrote deleteFile in src/core/database/persistence.js to iterate every fingerprint for the filepath and cascade per-fingerprint (same shape as pruneFilesNotIn). New getAllFilesByPath helper returns all matching rows ordered by birthTimestamp DESC; deleteFile now uses it to enumerate rows, then for each fingerprint runs deleteConnectionsForFile / deleteIntentForFile / deleteMutationLogForFile / deleteTicketsForFile (the new helper from ticket 9), then `DELETE FROM file_registry WHERE fingerprint = ?`. Return shape stays backwards-compatible: callers still see `.changes` and `.fingerprint` (now the newest one); new `.fingerprints` array surfaces the full set.

Verified end-to-end by running `node start.js .` under PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON — the orphan that the old deleteFile would have left behind would have FK-violated on the very next pruneFilesNotIn pass; instead the live st8.sqlite reports `foreign_key_check` = [] post-run.
reviewNote: Verified: deleteFile now uses getAllFilesByPath, iterates per-fingerprint, calls all four cascade helpers (connections / intent / mutation_log / tickets), and DELETEs by fingerprint, not filepath. Return shape preserves `.changes` + `.fingerprint`, adds `.fingerprints` array. Multi-fingerprint probe confirms newest wins in `.fingerprint` and FK check stays clean ([]) after the delete. Prominent JSDoc comment now records the foot-gun the userNote called out — the executor did the rewrite, not just a comment.

userNote: PRAGMA foreign_keys is NEVER turned ON in initialize(). The schema's FOREIGN KEY declarations are documentary only — SQLite will happily accept orphan rows in connections, file_intent, file_mutation_log, tickets. Manual JS-side cascade in deleteFile/pruneFilesNotIn is the only thing keeping referential integrity, and it has the deleteFile foot-gun above. Fix: enable foreign keys, then audit every existing INSERT path to confirm it doesn't break. Likely surface latent orphans on first turn-on.
actionsTaken:
Added `this.db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON')` to St8Persistence.initialize() in src/core/database/persistence.js immediately after the WAL/synchronous pragmas, before ST8_SCHEMA is applied.

First boot under enforcement surfaced exactly the orphan source the ticket warned about: pruneFilesNotIn threw SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_FOREIGNKEY at persistence.js:337 because tickets had no cascade entry (ticket 9). Resolved in the same commit (1363afe) by adding deleteTicketsForFile and wiring it into both cascade paths. After the fix:
  - PRAGMA foreign_key_check on the live st8.sqlite returns [].
  - PRAGMA integrity_check returns ok.
  - `node start.js .` completes a full indexer pass (281 → 283 files across two runs) with no FK errors.
  - verify-persistence-fixes 10/10.

No orphan sources outside this cluster were tripped — main.js's two-pass indexer correctly upserts file_registry rows before inserting connections, intent, and mutations. The activity_log writes use targetFingerprint without an FK by design.
residualConcerns: If a future writer ever logs a connection or mutation referencing a fingerprint that hasn't been upserted yet, FK enforcement will throw. The validator added to logActivity in ticket 3 is a model for the same pattern on logMutation / insertConnection; not in scope here.
reviewNote: Verified: `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON` is set in initialize() at L303 right after journal_mode/synchronous, before ST8_SCHEMA is applied. Live `x.db.pragma('foreign_keys')` returns `[{foreign_keys:1}]`; `foreign_key_check` returns []; integrity_check returns ok. Residual concern is honest: confirmed main.js's indexer upserts file_registry before logMutation/insertConnection at every call site (lines 129→145, 297→299, 350→352), so the latent-orphan risk is bounded to future writers. Inline comment in initialize() spells out why JS-side cascade is still needed (no ON DELETE CASCADE — deliberate, so mutation_log can be written before deletion).

userNote: RE-FLAG FROM DB ANGLE: activity_log.targetFingerprint is the camelCase column declared in ST8_SCHEMA L119. logActivity() reads activity.targetFingerprint (camelCase). But app.js:1558 _handleTickets logs with `target_fingerprint: payload.fingerprint` (snake_case). The fingerprint is silently nulled on every TICKET_CREATED activity row. Already in hooks-and-integration.json; re-raising here because the column naming convention is set by the schema and the bug pattern (snake vs camel) is one we will keep hitting unless the schema column itself is renamed to snake_case for consistency with SQLite norms, or a logActivity input-validator throws on unknown keys.
actionsTaken:
Two-part fix:

1. src/core/server/app.js:1558 — `target_fingerprint: payload.fingerprint` corrected to `targetFingerprint: payload.fingerprint`, matching the activity_log column and the four other logActivity call sites in app.js (lines 376, 729, 1473) and main.js (none use targetFingerprint, but the convention is uniform).

2. src/core/database/persistence.js logActivity — added an input-key validator. The method now whitelists {source, action, targetFingerprint, details} and throws a descriptive error on any unknown key. Verified the throw fires on the snake_case shape and not on the camelCase shape. The error message tells callers exactly what's wrong: '[st8:persistence] logActivity: unknown key(s) [target_fingerprint]. activity_log columns are camelCase — did you mean targetFingerprint?'

This is the cross-cluster ticket also flagged in hooks-and-integration.json. The DB-side fix (validator) is what makes this NOT recur — even if a future writer reflexively types snake_case, the call now throws immediately rather than silently NULLing the column.
residualConcerns: The hooks-and-integration cluster owns the original ticket; that cluster's reviewer should confirm the fix here resolves their version (the producer site) and decide whether to close out the duplicate or keep it as a paired record.
reviewNote: Verified: `grep target_fingerprint src/core/server/app.js` returns nothing — the snake_case was replaced with camelCase at L1558. logActivity now whitelists {source, action, targetFingerprint, details} and THROWS (not just logs) on unknown keys. Probed both directions in-memory: snake_case throws with the exact descriptive message the actionsTaken describes; camelCase passes through. This is the better answer than 'rename the schema' — it makes the bug pattern impossible to recur silently.

Per the batch 021 cleanup agent's directive (scripts go under scripts/), this should move to /home/user/st8/scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js. Internal require path is `./persistence` and would need to become `../src/core/database/persistence`.
actionsTaken:
Moved via `git mv src/core/database/verify-persistence-fixes.js scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js` (preserves rename history). Internal require updated from `./persistence` to `../src/core/database/persistence` to compensate for the new relative location.

One external reference required updating: scripts/migration/check-conventions.js:330 lists known-orphan entry points; updated the path there from src/core/database/verify-persistence-fixes.js to scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js so the orphan-detection pass continues to ignore the verifier (a script, not a library module, is expected to be an orphan).

No other live references in src/ or scripts/. scripts/migration/verify.js (the sub-process probe added in batch 010) doesn't hardcode the old path — it iterates over all migrated files generically. Doc references under .planning/ and st8_json/ are historical records of the original backend/ location and were left as-is per the bible's 'don't quietly delete' principle.

Verified: `node scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js` runs from the new location and reports `=== Results: 10 passed, 0 failed ===` — identical to pre-move behavior.
reviewNote: Verified: `git ls-files scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js` returns the path; `git ls-files src/core/database/verify-persistence-fixes.js` returns nothing — git rename history is preserved (97% similarity per git show stat). Internal require updated to `../src/core/database/persistence` (commit d53906f, line `const mod = require('../src/core/database/persistence');`). `node scripts/verify-persistence-fixes.js` from the new location reports `=== Results: 10 passed, 0 failed ===`. The check-conventions.js entry-points list update at line 330 is real and visible in the diff (old path → new path, single-line edit). Honest, surgical move.

userNote: ST8_SCHEMA is a 150-line template literal with no boot-time introspection. There is no helper that runs `PRAGMA table_info(...)` / `sqlite_master` and compares to expected columns. Drift between the schema string and the live st8.sqlite (e.g. ALTER-added columns that were never folded into ST8_SCHEMA, or vice-versa) is invisible until something queries the missing column and throws. Build a schema-introspection helper that logs diffs at boot — pairs naturally with the migration framework ticket.
actionsTaken:
Added a parallel EXPECTED_SCHEMA constant (src/core/database/persistence.js, sits directly above the St8Persistence class) listing every table and its expected column set. Added an introspectSchema() method that runs `SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'` plus `PRAGMA table_info(<table>)` for every expected table and returns a structured diff: { hasDrift, missingTables, extraTables, missingColumns, extraColumns, report }.

initialize() now calls introspectSchema() after applying ST8_SCHEMA and emits `[st8:persistence:drift]` warnings on any detected mismatch. Errors during introspection log and continue (the DB is still usable; we just lost the diff signal — no point in failing initialize() over a logging facility).

Why a parallel constant instead of parsing ST8_SCHEMA? Parsing a 150-line template literal at boot would be fragile (commented-out blocks, multi-line FK clauses, etc.). EXPECTED_SCHEMA is hand-maintained — anyone editing ST8_SCHEMA is expected to edit EXPECTED_SCHEMA in the same edit. The comment block on EXPECTED_SCHEMA spells this out.

Verified:
  - Fresh in-memory DB: hasDrift=false, report=[].
  - Live st8.sqlite (after the ticket 2 fix run): hasDrift=false.
  - Stunted DB probe (file_registry pared to two columns, rogue table injected): hasDrift=true, every expected table flagged missing, file_registry's 22 missing columns enumerated, the rogue table listed as extra.

Pairs with the future migration framework (P1.1, Wave 1B): the migration runner mutates the live DB, the introspector confirms the mutation landed.
residualConcerns: EXPECTED_SCHEMA only checks column NAMES, not types or defaults — a future change that renames a column's type from INTEGER to TEXT would not be caught. Type-checking is straightforward to add but was out of scope for this ticket's 'log diffs at boot' brief.
reviewNote: Verified: EXPECTED_SCHEMA constant lists all 9 tables, columns match the schema exactly. introspectSchema() is called at the tail of initialize() under a try/catch that logs+continues (correct — drift detection should never block boot). Probed live: fresh DB → hasDrift=false. Injected drift (ALTER ADD COLUMN bogusCol + CREATE TABLE rogue_table) → hasDrift=true and report enumerates both: `extra table (not in EXPECTED_SCHEMA): rogue_table` and `file_registry: extra column(s) not in EXPECTED_SCHEMA [bogusCol]`. Residual concern about type checking is honestly flagged. The catch block uses console.error (not silent) and re-uses driftErr.message — not a hidden swallow.

userNote: The maestro DatabasePersister fallthrough is silently degraded. graph-persister.js exports the class as `exports.DatabasePersister = DatabasePersister`, so the loaded module is the namespace object — `typeof === 'function'` is ALWAYS false, the maestro path NEVER runs, and we always reach the better-sqlite3 fallback. The log line `[st8:persistence] Using better-sqlite3 directly` reads as a normal success message — there is no signal that the primary path failed by design. Either (a) remove the dead branch entirely and document that st8 always uses better-sqlite3 direct, or (b) leave the branch but flip the log levels to make the fallback explicit ('maestro DatabasePersister not callable — falling back to better-sqlite3 direct').
actionsTaken:
Chose option (a) per persistence-and-database.md §6 + roadmap P2.6 — the cleaner finish.

Changes to src/core/database/persistence.js:
  - initialize() unconditionally constructs `new (require('better-sqlite3'))(this.dbPath)`. The new log line records the design rationale on every boot: '[st8:persistence] Initialised better-sqlite3 (st8.sqlite owns its own schema; maestro DatabasePersister is project-scoped to scaffolder_data.sqlite)'.
  - Dropped the dead loadLibModule, getDatabasePersister, and _databasePersister helpers (and the now-unused `fs` import). They had no callers outside the dead initialize() try-block.
  - graph-persister.js is left in place because insight-store imports it separately for getSharedDatabasePath() (a different DB file).
  - Added a comment in the LIB CODE REFERENCES section recording why the helpers were removed, so a future reader doesn't try to re-add them.

Verified: in-memory init prints the new log line; verify-persistence-fixes 10/10; `node start.js .` indexes 283 files end-to-end without error.
reviewNote: Verified: executor chose option (a) — branch removed, not just log-leveled. Confirmed by diff: loadLibModule, getDatabasePersister, _databasePersister, and the unused `fs` import are all gone. initialize() unconditionally constructs better-sqlite3. The new log line records the design intent in-line, exactly as the userNote asked for ('design intent now obvious in the file'). graph-persister.js correctly left in place for insight-store's getSharedDatabasePath() — separate DB. The replacement LIB CODE REFERENCES comment block explains the removal so a future reader doesn't re-introduce the dead branch.

userNote: ai_content has no FK to file_registry — it stores filepath as freeform TEXT. Renames, deletions, and re-creations of files leave ai_content rows pointing at stale paths with no cascade. Was this intentional (content may arrive before the file is indexed) or an oversight? Decide and document. If intentional: add a comment on the table block explaining the design. If oversight: add filepath OR fingerprint FK and a cascade hook in deleteFile/pruneFilesNotIn.
actionsTaken:
Decision: INTENTIONAL, loose-coupling is correct.

Rationale:
  - grep'd the codebase — storeAIContent / getAIContent currently have NO callers outside their own definitions in persistence.js. The methods are a v0 surface for the @@@ symbol pipeline.
  - Workflow shape: content can arrive before the file is indexed (e.g. a transcript referencing a file that's about to be created) AND content should outlive the file (deleting the file shouldn't erase the historical record of what was said about that path).
  - Filepath-only join key reinforces the looseness — even if the file is later re-created with a different fingerprint, the content is keyed to the path.

Action: documented the design in a prominent ST8_SCHEMA comment block above the ai_content CREATE TABLE statement (src/core/database/persistence.js). The comment spells out:
  - WHY no FK (content-first workflow, content outlives the file).
  - That ai_content is deliberately NOT in the deleteFile / pruneFilesNotIn cascade.
  - The exact checklist for reversing this decision if a future contributor wants tight coupling: (a) add fingerprint column + FK, (b) wire cascade hook in two places, (c) update EXPECTED_SCHEMA, (d) write a backfill migration.

Follow-up correction: the initial commit (8f0b27c) used backticks inside the schema comment, which JS parsed as template-string terminators and the module failed to load. Replaced with plain prose in commit 3396fb7. Net change is comment-only; no SQL behavior or code path changed.
residualConcerns: If callers materialize (the @@@ pipeline wires up), revisit. The decision is recorded but not load-bearing yet.
reviewNote: Verified: 19-line schema-block comment sits directly above the ai_content CREATE TABLE in ST8_SCHEMA. Real explanation, not a one-liner — covers (a) WHY filepath-not-fingerprint, (b) WHY no FK, (c) WHY NOT in cascade, plus an explicit four-step reversal checklist. Caller-audit claim is correct: `grep storeAIContent|appendAIContent` returns only the two definitions inside persistence.js — no external consumers exist, so the loose-coupling decision has no immediate blast radius. Note: the 8f0b27c→3396fb7 backtick-escape fix is honest — executor surfaced their own intermediate-commit mistake in the actionsTaken, exactly the kind of disclosure that builds trust.

userNote: tickets.claimedBy is freeform TEXT with no enum/no providers table. createTicket and claimTicket accept any string ('anthropic', 'openai', 'Anthropic', 'unknown', typos). Aggregations by provider have no constraint help. If we expect to scale the LLM-collaborator channel, introduce a `providers (id, name, kind, ...)` table and make claimedBy a FK. Today's freeform approach is fine for the MVP but will hurt the moment a UI tries to filter open tickets by provider.
actionsTaken:
Implemented the persistence-side half of the work (table + seeder + CRUD + JS-side FK validator). Left the SQL-level FOREIGN KEY declaration on tickets.claimedBy out by design — see below.

Changes to src/core/database/persistence.js:

1. New `providers` table appended to ST8_SCHEMA: `id` (TEXT PK), `displayName`, `kind` ∈ {cloud, local, human, custom}, `envKey` (API-key env var name, nullable), `docsUrl` (nullable), `active` (INTEGER DEFAULT 1), `createdAt`, `updatedAt`. Index on `active`. Block comment above the table explains the no-SQL-FK design choice and the cross-cluster handoff to settings-and-providers Wave 5b.

2. New `CANONICAL_PROVIDERS` constant mirrors LLM_PROVIDERS in src/frontend/components/settings/settings.js (the same 7 entries: anthropic, openai, google, ollama, lmstudio, openrouter, custom) plus an 8th — 'human' — to represent a human collaborator claiming a ticket. The two registries are intended to stay in sync; the schema-block comment records the dependency.

3. EXPECTED_SCHEMA updated with the providers row so introspectSchema() can verify the table landed cleanly. Boot of an in-memory DB confirms hasDrift=false; boot against the live st8.sqlite confirms providers table was created and seeded (8 rows: anthropic,custom,google,human,lmstudio,ollama,openai,openrouter) and foreign_key_check returns [].

4. New methods on St8Persistence: seedCanonicalProviders() (INSERT OR IGNORE — keeps user-edited rows intact), getProvider(id), getAllProviders({activeOnly}), upsertProvider(p) (validates id/displayName/kind required, kind enum), deactivateProvider(id) (soft delete only — hard delete would orphan historical tickets.claimedBy references).

5. initialize() calls seedCanonicalProviders() after introspectSchema(), wrapped in try/catch so a seed failure can't block boot.

6. claimTicket(id, claimedBy) rewritten to throw on unknown/inactive providers — same pattern as Wave 1A's logActivity key-whitelist validator. Three rejection paths: empty/non-string claimedBy → TypeError; unknown id → RangeError listing all known providers; active=0 → RangeError pointing at upsertProvider for re-activation.

Why no SQL-level FOREIGN KEY on tickets.claimedBy:
  - tickets is `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` and predates providers on every existing st8.sqlite. SQLite won't add an FK column to an existing table without a 12-step table recreation — which requires the migration framework (ticket 0, deferred).
  - JS-side enforcement on the single mutation method (claimTicket) is equivalent for correctness because no other path writes claimedBy. The bible's logActivity validator pattern is the precedent.
  - When the migration framework lands, the recommended migration is: rename old tickets → tickets_old, CREATE TABLE tickets with FOREIGN KEY (claimedBy) REFERENCES providers(id), INSERT INTO tickets SELECT * FROM tickets_old, DROP tickets_old. Block comment in the providers schema records this.

Verified end-to-end:
  - seeded providers on fresh DB: 8 rows.
  - claimTicket('anthropic') on a valid ticket: changes=1.
  - claimTicket('totally-fake'): throws RangeError 'unknown provider'.
  - claimTicket(''): throws TypeError 'non-empty string'.
  - claimTicket('openai') after deactivation: throws RangeError 'inactive'.
  - upsertProvider({kind:'rocket'}): throws RangeError on invalid kind.
  - upsertProvider({id:'mistral', displayName:'Mistral', kind:'cloud'}): inserts; getProvider returns the row.
  - Live st8.sqlite: seeded 8 providers, no drift, foreign_key_check returns [], verify-persistence-fixes 10/10.
residualConcerns: (1) Cross-cluster: the settings-and-providers cluster (Wave 5b) owns the CRUD UI that should wire to getAllProviders / upsertProvider / deactivateProvider. Until that lands, providers can only be edited via the persistence API directly (no HTTP endpoint exposes them yet). (2) Hard SQL FK on tickets.claimedBy → providers.id is deferred to whoever ships ticket 0's migration framework — block comment in ST8_SCHEMA records the recommended migration recipe. (3) src/frontend/components/settings/settings.js LLM_PROVIDERS is still the canonical frontend constant; ideally a future commit removes the duplication by having the frontend fetch /api/providers (settings cluster's call).
reviewNote: Verified end-to-end: providers table is in ST8_SCHEMA with the documented columns (id PK, displayName, kind, envKey, docsUrl, active, createdAt, updatedAt) + idx_providers_active. CANONICAL_PROVIDERS constant mirrors the 7 frontend LLM_PROVIDERS entries plus 'human' = 8 rows. Live probe: in-memory init seeds all 8 (anthropic, custom, google, human, lmstudio, ollama, openai, openrouter), claimTicket('nonsense-provider') throws RangeError with the descriptive message listing all 8 known providers, claimTicket(validId, 'anthropic') succeeds (changes=1). The rationale comment for the deferred SQL-level FK is present in ST8_SCHEMA above the providers DDL (cites ticket 0's migration framework as the gate, names the 12-step recreate as the future migration recipe). EXPECTED_SCHEMA row for providers exists. residualConcern (3) about the settings.js:44 LLM_PROVIDERS duplication is real — confirmed by reading the frontend file: lines 44-53 are the duplicated registry the executor flagged. The JS-side validator pattern matches Wave 1A's logActivity validator — loud-bug-beats-quiet-bug propagated correctly.

userNote: tickets has no cascade entry in deleteFile or pruneFilesNotIn. Deleting a file_registry row leaves its tickets with a dangling FK. Currently safe only because PRAGMA foreign_keys is off and ticket queries don't JOIN on file_registry. Fix: add deleteTicketsForFile(fingerprint) and call it from both cascade points — same shape as deleteIntentForFile / deleteMutationLogForFile.
actionsTaken:
Added deleteTicketsForFile(fingerprint) to St8Persistence (src/core/database/persistence.js), shape identical to deleteIntentForFile / deleteMutationLogForFile. Wired into both cascade paths:
  - deleteFile(): called inside the per-fingerprint loop of the rewritten implementation.
  - pruneFilesNotIn(): called inside the existing per-fingerprint loop alongside the other three cascade helpers.

This was the orphan-creating bug that fired SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_FOREIGNKEY on the first boot after enabling foreign_keys = ON (ticket 2). The stack pinpointed pruneFilesNotIn at persistence.js:337 — the live st8.sqlite had one ticket whose file_registry row was about to be pruned. Adding the cascade resolved the throw in the same commit (1363afe). Post-fix: live DB foreign_key_check = [].
reviewNote: Verified: deleteTicketsForFile(fingerprint) helper exists at L659 in persistence.js with the same shape as deleteIntentForFile / deleteMutationLogForFile. Wired into BOTH cascade paths: deleteFile inside its per-fingerprint loop, and pruneFilesNotIn (diff at L399). The verify-persistence-fixes script doesn't yet probe ticket cascade specifically, but the in-memory probe above (create file → createTicket → deleteFile → foreign_key_check returns []) confirms the cascade works under FK enforcement. Co-bundled with ticket 1/2/13 in 1363afe — the executor was honest about the coupling and the trace that exposed the orphan.

userNote: file_mutation_log will grow unbounded. Append-only, no retention policy, no automatic rotation. purgeDevelopmentData exists for the DEVELOPMENT→PRODUCTION lifecycle transition but is never called automatically. Long-lived projects will see this table balloon. Define a retention policy (e.g. keep all PRODUCTION/PURGE mutations forever, prune CONCEPT/dev mutations older than N days) and wire it as a periodic INDEX_COMPLETE subscriber.
actionsTaken:
Policy implemented exactly as the userNote sketches:
  - KEEP FOREVER: mutationType in ('PRODUCTION', 'PURGE') — lifecycle-transition markers must remain queryable for the project's lifetime.
  - PRUNE AFTER N: every other mutationType (CONCEPT and content-mutation strings) older than retentionDays=30 days (default, parameterised).

Two-part implementation:

1. src/core/database/persistence.js — new method pruneMutationLogRetention(retentionDays=30). Computes a cutoff ISO timestamp matching SQLite CURRENT_TIMESTAMP format ('YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS', no TZ — SQLite compares ISO strings chronologically). DELETE WHERE mutationType NOT IN ('PRODUCTION','PURGE') AND timestamp < cutoff. Returns {prunedRows, retentionDays, cutoff} so the caller can log without re-querying. Validates retentionDays is non-negative finite — throws RangeError otherwise (loud bug pattern from Wave 1A).

2. src/core/hooks/default-subscribers.js — new P=50 subscriber on HOOKS.INDEX_COMPLETE (slots between intent-seeder P=40 and force-checks P=90). Wraps the prune call in:
  - Method-presence guard (no-op if persistence lacks pruneMutationLogRetention — defensive against older persistence instances).
  - 24-hour gate via st8_settings ('persistence' / 'mutationLogLastPruneAt' = Date.now()). The indexer can run many times per day during active development; running the prune once per calendar day is the right cadence.
  - try/catch isolation so a prune failure can't poison the rest of the INDEX_COMPLETE chain.
  - Single log line: '[st8] file_mutation_log retention: pruned N row(s) older than 30d (cutoff <ISO>)'.

Why P=50 (not part of P=40 intent-seeder or P=90 force-check):
  - P=10..40 build/read mutation_log (intent-seeder reads recent mutations). Retention must NOT run before they finish or it can race with their queries.
  - P=90 force-checks reads file_registry / connections / schema cards — independent surfaces. Retention should land before force-check so its output reflects the post-retention state.
  - P=50 sits cleanly in the middle: post-write, pre-cross-tool-verification.

Verified end-to-end on the live /home/user/st8/st8.sqlite:
  - First boot after install: '[st8] file_mutation_log retention: pruned 0 row(s) older than 30d (cutoff 2026-04-15 ...)' — correctly emits because the gate setting didn't exist yet. mutationLogLastPruneAt is now written.
  - Second boot immediately after: no retention line — gate triggered, skipped silently. Correct behavior.
  - Synthetic probe: injected one old CONCEPT (60d ago) + one old PRODUCTION (60d ago) against a real fingerprint, cleared the gate, called pruneMutationLogRetention(30) directly — result.prunedRows=1, survivor mutationType='PRODUCTION'. Policy correctly preserves PRODUCTION while pruning CONCEPT.
  - verify-persistence-fixes 10/10 from new location.
  - foreign_key_check on live DB after retention run: [] (clean).
residualConcerns: Default retentionDays=30 is hardcoded in default-subscribers.js. A future ticket might surface this as a setting under st8_settings('persistence','mutationLogRetentionDays') so users can tune it without editing source. Out of scope for the 'wire as periodic subscriber' brief — the userNote specified the policy shape, not a configuration UI.
reviewNote: Verified deeply — this was the trickiest ticket to audit. pruneMutationLogRetention exists in persistence.js with the correct WHERE clause: `DELETE FROM file_mutation_log WHERE mutationType NOT IN ('PRODUCTION', 'PURGE') AND timestamp < ?`. Note the userNote in the reviewer brief sketched `lifecyclePhase IN ('CONCEPT','DEVELOPMENT','CONTENT')` but the persistence component doc §2.4 explicitly establishes mutationType (not lifecyclePhase) as the dimension that takes values CONCEPT/PRODUCTION/PURGE/content-strings — executor chose the doc-aligned dimension and got the inverse-filter logic right. Synthetic probe (old CONCEPT + old PRODUCTION + new CONCEPT, all on a real fingerprint, called pruneMutationLogRetention(30) directly): prunedRows=1, old PRODUCTION preserved, new CONCEPT preserved. Policy correct. P=50 priority confirmed in default-subscribers.js between intent-seeder (P=40) and force-checks (P=90). The 24h gate is REAL persistence-backed via `getSetting('persistence','mutationLogLastPruneAt') + upsertSetting(...)` — NOT a hardcoded boot-time variable that resets on every run (which would have been the stub failure mode). try/catch around the whole subscriber surfaces failures via console.error (not silent swallow), with method-presence guard for defensive compat. The hardcoded-30-days residualConcern is honest and out-of-scope per the original brief.

userNote: prd_projects table — what's its current state? Schema looks complete (name UNIQUE, JSON variables, created/updated). createPRDProject/getPRDProject/getAllPRDProjects/updatePRDProject/deletePRDProject all exist. But is anyone calling them? Audit the consumers; if dormant, mark for either deletion or wire-up.
actionsTaken:
Audit verdict: LIVE — do not delete.

Grep across src/ and start.js for the five method names + the table name surfaces a live end-to-end wiring:

  Frontend → API → Persistence:
    - src/frontend/index.html:143 — <button class="prd-btn-create" id="prd-create-btn" onclick="createPRDProject()">CREATE PROJECT</button> in the PRD panel.
    - src/frontend/app.js:277 — window.createPRDProject() implementation (posts to the API).
    - src/core/server/app.js:1086 — _handlePrdProjects services three HTTP routes:
        GET  /api/prd-projects           → persistence.getAllPRDProjects()
        GET  /api/prd-projects/:name     → persistence.getPRDProject(name)
        POST /api/prd-projects           → persistence.createPRDProject(name, projectPath, template, variables)
    - src/core/database/persistence.js — all five CRUD methods (definitions only).

updatePRDProject and deletePRDProject have NO HTTP routes yet — that's PRD-UI work, not a persistence concern. Keeping the methods means the PRD feature can grow without re-touching this file.

Action taken: documented the wiring in a prominent block comment above the prd_projects CREATE TABLE statement in ST8_SCHEMA (src/core/database/persistence.js). The comment records: which frontend button/handler invokes the table, which API routes service it, which methods are wired vs reserved-for-future-UI-work, and the cross-cluster note that the PRD generator at src/features/prd/ also uses this table. Future readers running the same audit will find the answer in-place instead of repeating the grep.

No code-path changes — pure documentation. Verified the module still loads (`introspectSchema()` returns hasDrift=false post-edit).
residualConcerns: If a future refactor consolidates PRD storage to a different location (e.g. the PRD feature gets its own DB), revisit this comment and either move the audit trail to the new home or delete the table + methods cleanly. The block comment notes this dependency.
reviewNote: Verified the LIVE verdict. The 22-line wiring trail block comment sits above the prd_projects CREATE TABLE in ST8_SCHEMA (git stat confirms exactly 22 insertions). Independent grep audit confirms every claim in the comment: src/frontend/index.html:143 has the CREATE PROJECT button → `onclick="createPRDProject()"`; src/frontend/app.js:277 has `window.createPRDProject = async function()`; src/core/server/app.js:127 + 1088 handle `/api/prd-projects` routes (GET list / GET :name / POST create) wired to getAllPRDProjects/getPRDProject/createPRDProject. The 'reserved for future UI' claim for updatePRDProject and deletePRDProject is honest — `grep -rn 'updatePRDProject\|deletePRDProject' src/ scripts/` returns only the method definitions in persistence.js plus the documentation comment itself. Zero external callers, confirming the audit. Pure documentation commit; no code-path change.

userNote: storeAIContent comment says 'INSERT OR REPLACE' but there is no UNIQUE constraint on the ai_content table — every call is effectively an INSERT and rows accumulate. If the intent is one-row-per-filepath, add `UNIQUE(filepath)` to the schema. If the intent is a history of injections, the comment is misleading — rename the method to appendAIContent and remove the OR REPLACE.
actionsTaken:
Chose option (b): append semantics, rename. Aligns with the ai_content design decision recorded in ticket 7 (ai_content is a HISTORY of injections — content-first, content-outlives-file).

Changes to src/core/database/persistence.js:
  - New canonical method appendAIContent(filepath, content): plain INSERT, no OR REPLACE.
  - storeAIContent(filepath, content): retained as a @deprecated thin alias that just delegates to appendAIContent. Kept because the docs reference the old name and any future caller written against the documented API shouldn't silently break.
  - JSDoc on both methods explains the rename + the why.

Verified: appendAIContent('/foo.js') + appendAIContent('/foo.js') + storeAIContent('/foo.js') produces three rows from getAIContent. Note: behavior is functionally unchanged (rows always accumulated — OR REPLACE was a no-op with no UNIQUE constraint); the rename just makes the existing behavior honest.
reviewNote: Verified: appendAIContent exists at L1013 (plain INSERT, no OR REPLACE). storeAIContent retained at L1026 as `@deprecated` thin alias that calls appendAIContent — userNote allowed either option; keeping the alias is the lower-risk choice. Caller audit: grep across the repo confirms NO external consumers needed updating, so 'all internal callers updated' is vacuously true. JSDoc on both methods is honest about the design (append-log semantics align with the ticket-7 ai_content decision).

userNote: getFileByPath uses no ORDER BY. With multiple rows per filepath (different fingerprints from different runs), the returned row is non-deterministic (physical layout order). Every consumer of getFileByPath assumes 'the' file — but there may be more than one. Fix: either ORDER BY birthTimestamp DESC (most recent) or document the multi-row possibility and return an array. Affects deleteFile (see related ticket) and ~10 server.js call sites.
actionsTaken:
Two-method fix in src/core/database/persistence.js:

1. getFileByPath now uses `ORDER BY birthTimestamp DESC` — most-recent identity wins deterministically. The JSDoc spells out the multi-row possibility and points callers that need to operate on every fingerprint at getAllFilesByPath.

2. New getAllFilesByPath(filepath) returns every row for a filepath, ordered by birthTimestamp DESC. deleteFile (ticket 1) uses it to enumerate fingerprints for the per-fingerprint cascade.

Why DESC and not document-and-return-array: option (1) preserves the existing call-site assumption (one row) for the ~10 server.js consumers — they all want 'the current file at this path', which is unambiguously the newest birthTimestamp. Switching to an array return would have forced changes across every consumer. Option (2) — getAllFilesByPath — gives callers that NEED the full set an explicit way to ask.
reviewNote: Verified: getFileByPath SQL is `SELECT * FROM file_registry WHERE filepath = ? ORDER BY birthTimestamp DESC` (uses .get() so still returns one row — the newest). New getAllFilesByPath returns .all() with the same ORDER BY. Multi-fingerprint probe confirms behavior: two rows inserted with different birthTimestamps → getFileByPath returns the 2025 one, getAllFilesByPath returns both newest-first. The 'preserve existing one-row consumer assumption' design choice (rather than refactor ~10 server.js call sites to handle arrays) is defended in the actionsTaken and is the right call for a minimum-blast-radius fix.

userNote: graph-persister.js was generated by tsc (see __createBinding/__importStar boilerplate at top). It's a compiled JS artifact, not a hand-edited source file. The .ts source presumably lives in maestro-scaffolder-tool. Decide: vendor the .ts so future edits don't get clobbered, or treat this file as read-only and document that any change must round-trip through the upstream .ts. Today's state risks divergence with no recorded provenance.
actionsTaken:
Decision: READ-ONLY vendored artifact (option b in the userNote). Documented provenance both in the file header AND in repo-level metadata.

Why not vendor the .ts source: the upstream lives in maestro-scaffolder-tool, which is a separate repo with its own build pipeline. Pulling the .ts in would require either (1) vendoring tsc + tsconfig + the bridging types as well (a non-trivial build surface to maintain in this repo), or (2) leaving the .ts to drift away from the upstream — defeating the purpose. The cleaner finish is to mark this file as generated, document where the source lives, and ensure tooling treats it as such.

Caller audit confirmed the file is load-bearing — grep shows seven live importers across src/:
  - src/features/search/sonic-indexer.js:22
  - src/features/search/sonic-queries.js:57
  - src/features/graph/traversal.js:65
  - src/features/integr8/index.js:55
  - src/features/indexing/parser-persistence.js:45
  - src/features/analysis/insight-store.js:46
  - src/features/indexing/background-indexer.js:60
  - src/features/indexing/indexer.js:56 (dynamic load)
Deleting or renaming the file would break the integr8 + sonic pipelines. persistence.js (st8.sqlite's owner) does NOT import it — the maestro-DatabasePersister fallthrough was removed in ticket 6.

Two concrete artifacts shipped:

1. New file header in src/core/database/graph-persister.js — 34-line block comment marking the file GENERATED, naming the upstream path (maestro-scaffolder-tool/src/commands/integr8/databasePersister.ts), explaining the tsc compile (the __createBinding / __importStar boilerplate at top + the trailing source-map footer at line 229 are the tell-tales), stating READ-ONLY status, listing every live importer's purpose, and pointing at the .gitattributes entry. The original two-line tsc header is preserved as the last comment line so the link back to the .ts file isn't lost.

2. New /home/user/st8/.gitattributes (file didn't exist) marking src/core/database/graph-persister.js with linguist-generated=true. This collapses the diff in GitHub PR review tools and excludes the file from language statistics — the standard signal that a file is generated/vendored rather than hand-written.

Verified: graph-persister still loads cleanly (`require(...)` returns {DatabasePersister: function, getSharedDatabasePath: function}); no runtime change.
residualConcerns: If the maestro-scaffolder-tool upstream ships a breaking change to DatabasePersister or getSharedDatabasePath, the seven importers under src/features/ will silently break until someone re-vendors this file. There is no automated sync. Mitigation: when re-vendoring, re-run `node start.js .` and check `[st8:force-check] N/N pass` — the cross-tool integrity checks will surface most contract drift.
reviewNote: Verified: graph-persister.js now opens with a block-comment header (the executor described it as 34-line; the actual block runs lines 2-34 inclusive — fits the description). The header documents upstream source location (maestro-scaffolder-tool/src/commands/integr8/databasePersister.ts), regeneration process (tsc, witnessed by the __createBinding boilerplate + the trailing source-map footer), READ-ONLY status, every live importer's purpose, and points at .gitattributes. /home/user/st8/.gitattributes exists with `src/core/database/graph-persister.js linguist-generated=true`. The 7-importer claim verified independently via `grep -rn 'require.*graph-persister' src/`: sonic-indexer:22, sonic-queries:57, traversal:65, integr8/index:55, insight-store:46, parser-persistence:45, background-indexer:60 — exactly seven live require sites under src/features/, matching the actionsTaken list. The persistence.js comment-match is a doc reference (not an importer), correctly excluded. The original tsc 2-line header is preserved as the final comment line in the block. Honest, well-scoped vendor-marking work.
